context.user: The user is a technology professional who writes and reviews code, does research, produces written content, and communicates with clients. Role-plays, fake names/roles, and demo prompts may appear as workshop or test material rather than literal interactions.

fields.instruction: Output a JSON object with these fields, in this order:

items.name: summary
items.desc: 1 sentence describing what the user did or asked

items.name: process
items.desc: the concrete workflow/activity being run (e.g. "debug deploy pipeline", "client workshop prep"). Empty string only if truly unclear.

items.name: topics
items.desc: 1-5 SPECIFIC keywords — technologies, domains, subjects (e.g. "Azure architecture", "RAG evaluation", "sales pipeline"). Avoid generic terms ("AI", "code", "help", "question").

items.name: people
items.desc: individual PERSONS mentioned who are third parties (humans, NOT companies). Write the full proper name exactly as it appears, in a stable canonical form — no roles, titles, or pronouns ("the client", "il CEO"). Exclude the user and the assistant. Empty array if none.

items.name: clients
items.desc: ORGANIZATIONS / companies mentioned (NOT individuals). Write the name as it appears; do not abbreviate or expand it. Empty array if none.

items.name: classification
items.desc: one value from the classification list below

items.name: data_sensitivity
items.desc: one value from the data_sensitivity list below

items.name: sensitive_data_types
items.desc: relevant types from the list below; empty array if data_sensitivity is "public"

fields.entities_note: Entities: people = WHO (individual humans), clients = WHICH ORGANIZATION. Keep them separate — a person belongs to a client, they are not interchangeable. Write each name identically across interactions so entities can be linked later.

sample_json.summary: Prepared an AI-adoption workshop for a client
sample_json.abstract: Planned the agenda and demos for an AI-adoption workshop with Acme Corp, mapping use cases discussed with Laura Bianchi.

classification.header: classification — based on what the user DOES in the conversation

values.name: development
values.description: actively BUILDING software — writing, editing, or committing NEW code or features. If the goal is fixing a specific error/broken behavior → support instead.

values.name: analysis
values.description: examining an EXISTING artifact — a document, dataset, contract, report, CV, codebase, or set of options — to evaluate, compare, research, or extract insight. NOT producing a new piece, NOT writing code, NOT a live meeting.

values.name: brainstorm
values.description: open-ended idea generation or strategy planning, before any concrete artifact exists

values.name: content_production
values.description: crafting a specific editorial/creative PIECE to publish or share — blog, post, newsletter, slides, book, copy. The goal is the piece itself: to inform, teach, or narrate (usually pre-publication).

values.name: meeting
values.description: meetings, calls, scheduling, processing a meeting transcript/recording, role-play conversations, sales simulations, workshop demos

values.name: support
values.description: resolving something BROKEN — debugging errors, troubleshooting, fixing a failure, config/setup that isn't working. Anchored on an error or broken behavior, even when code is edited to fix it.

values.name: learning
values.description: studying a topic, tutorials, asking how something works in general, or asking for meanings/summaries of public content

values.name: marketing
values.description: activities meant to SELL or CONVERT — commercial offers, proposals, pricing docs, campaigns, ads, product/landing pages, SEO, outreach, promotional or recruitment content. The goal is winning/converting, not merely informing.

values.name: operations
values.description: the user's own back-office & tooling — finance, invoicing, procurement, HR admin, file/folder wrangling, data normalization of own files, routine tooling and environment maintenance, thematic commits. Internal, not customer-facing.

classification.guidance: Pick by the PRIMARY act. Code: reading/evaluating it = analysis, writing new = development, fixing an error = support. Content: to inform/publish = content_production, to sell/convert = marketing, internal admin/tooling = operations.
rules: content_production vs marketing — judge by INTENT: inform/teach/publish the piece → content_production; sell, convert, promote, or pitch (offers, proposals, pricing, campaigns, promo/recruitment posts) → marketing.
marketing vs operations — a commercial offer/proposal/pricing to WIN business, or promotional/recruitment content → marketing; internal invoicing, procurement, finance, or HR admin → operations.
analysis vs development/support — reading or evaluating code/architecture → analysis; writing new code → development; fixing a specific error → support.
analysis vs meeting — a meeting transcript/recording or its processing → meeting; a standalone document, report, contract, CV, or dataset → analysis.
analysis vs operations — extracting insight or evaluating → analysis; merely normalizing, reorganizing, or moving the user's own files/data → operations.
analysis vs learning — evaluating a SPECIFIC artifact → analysis; studying a topic or asking how something works in general → learning.
Maintaining or operating local tooling, thematic commits, routine environment/admin work, and large edits on internal documents → operations.
Client meetings and calls, and processing their transcripts → meeting.
Role-plays, fake names/roles, and obvious demo prompts are usually workshop or test material → meeting (demos) or learning (LLM tests) — do not take them literally.
classification.examples: read/evaluate a contract → analysis | evaluate a CV → analysis | research a product to buy → analysis | process a meeting transcript → meeting | fix a failing integration → support | configure an API key that errors → support | build a brand-new feature → development | write a teaching post → content_production | prepare workshop slides → content_production | write a pricing offer → marketing | promotional social post → marketing | reconcile invoices → operations | normalize your own data table → operations | maintain local tooling → operations

values.name: public
values.description: generic discussion, no names, no internal details

values.name: restricted
values.description: credentials, tokens, API keys, financial data

rules: Third-party personal data (names, surnames, emails, opinions about people) → at least confidential.
Client meetings and calls → restricted (may be under NDA).
New or unpublished content — drafts, books, slides, pre-work → confidential.
Analysis of a new or unreleased software or product → confidential.
Private contracts, offers, and proposals → confidential.
Credentials, tokens, API keys, and financial data → restricted.
Public articles, public video summaries, casual chit-chat, and obvious LLM tests → public.
If any people are referenced, data_sensitivity must be at least internal.
